A significant low has three possible definitions. 1) The prior days low 2) A multi-hour low/ a low that goes 20+ points or 3) A cluster or shelf of lows. At ~6750 we had #1. This was the low of day for Wednesday and the flush and recovery of this would be highly actionable IF we even got it.

By 7:30PM we hit 6716. ES spent some time sitting here (for a full hour basically). I don’t directly bid supports (only Failed Breakdowns) but for those who like these this level offered a good 20 points.

prose_context.text: Readers were well prepared. I warned last Thursday at 4pm: “Watch for the Failed Breakdown of Tuesday’s 6718 daily low.” On Friday ES flushed last Tuesday’s 6718 low by a few points, recovered, and ripped. This bounce took ES to backtest that 6778-82 zone which was resistance all day Friday.

However, readers know that ES rarely travels in a straight line for long. What it does is it goes elevator down in a vertical in a vertical flush cutting through every support. Then, it flushes a big low, traps shorts, recovers, and price squeezes. This is my core setup: The Failed Breakdown. We saw this on Friday and we saw it again today.

On Friday however, this range finally broke down. This 6782 support was big support on Wednesday and Thursday last week. I wrote on Thursday at 4pm that: “Bear case Tomorrow: Begins below 6782.” Friday morning, we lost it, and down we went.

This week (until Friday) was a classic day traders market as ES is stuck trading in a large range which has been mostly 6782 to 6873 . Inside this, its been big fast sells that last for a few hours or half a day, followed by equally big squeezes the other way, caused by a backdrop of war headlines. For day traders, this action is prime, but its only prime...

article_title: Bulls Bought The Dip With Force. Dead Cat Bounce, or Bottom? March 10th Plan

prose_context.text: 1) Readers know that I talk everyday about the two siblings in ES. These siblings are elevator down sell and short squeeze, they are always paired together, and this is the tendency we exploit to generate our entries. Elevator down sell refers to a sharp flush where ES cuts through many supports with ease. This is usually how ES sells off. It rallies slow...

We saw it Friday morning. ES went elevator down from 6800+ to 6715.75 by 9:50AM. This was a fast, classic elevator down sell. What comes next? The short squeeze. By selling to 6715.75, ES lost Tuesday’s 6718.75 daily low. Shortly after, it recovered (Failed Breakdown) and the parabolic rip began to 6778-82 where we peaked Friday afternoon.

Readers were well prepared. I warned last Thursday at 4pm: “Watch for the Failed Breakdown of Tuesday’s 6718 daily low.” On Friday ES flushed last Tuesday’s 6718 low by a few points, recovered, and ripped. This bounce took ES to backtest that 6778-82 zone which was resistance all day Friday.

However, readers know that ES rarely travels in a straight line for long. What it does is it goes elevator down in a vertical in a vertical flush cutting through every support. Then, it flushes a big low, traps shorts, recovers, and price squeezes. This is my core setup: The Failed Breakdown. We saw this on Friday and we saw it again today.

Remember a Failed Breakdown requires price to lose and recover a significant low.

A significant low has three possible definitions. 1) The prior days low 2) A multi-hour low/ a low that goes 20+ points or 3) A cluster or shelf of lows. At ~6680 on November 24th we set a massive low at 6680 from which we rallied for months. Its the exact kind of low we’d want to trade the recovery of IF and when it triggered. It did not unfortunately.

article_title: Another Big Dip In SPX That Was Again Bought. Was That The Bottom? March 4 Plan

prose_context.text: Trade Recap/Daily Summary

After a big rally I sit back, do nothing, and wait for lots of deep red to appear - readers know my trading is simple. I trade only after an elevator down sell. I don’t trade chop, I don’t trade indecision. I trade fast, deep red. We finally got this elevator down into 9pm after lots of chop around 6873 support.
Why is 6853 a Failed Breakdown? Remember a Failed Breakdown requires price to lose and recover a significant low.

A significant low has three possible definitions. 1) The prior days low 2) A multi-hour low/ a low that goes 20+ points or 3) A cluster or shelf of lows. At ~6853 we had #2. At 11:30AM yesterday we set a huge low there, from which we rallied to 6912 high of day. This is a big low, the sort of which we’d want to see a flush and recovery of.

At 3:15PM on Wednesday, ES set a huge low there from which we rallied 30 points. This is a clear, V-shaped low of the sort we’d want to see a flush and recovery of.

article_title: SPX Is Again Coiled Tightly. Move Incoming. What Way? Feb 20th Plan

prose_context.text: Why 6872? Remember a Failed Breakdown requires price to lose and recover a significant low.

The first obvious Failed Breakdown was the Failed Breakdown of the 6872 3:15PM low (assuming we even got it as a trigger). I provided this setup yesterday at 4pm when I wrote: “The first proper support down is 6877. We held this late day today at 3:15PM more or less. It is well tested now. While one could try giving it another bid, remember my core rule: ...

A significant low has three possible definitions. 1) The prior days low 2) A multi-hour low/ a low that goes 20+ points or 3) A cluster or shelf of lows. At ~6872 we had #2. At 3:15PM on Wednesday, ES set a huge low there from which we rallied 30 points. This is a clear, V-shaped low of the sort we’d want to see a flush and recovery of.

As always with Failed Breakdowns though the golden rule is if you just rush in long on the recovery of the significant low, you lose. Prior to entry you HAVE TO see either: 1) One of the two acceptance types shown below. Acceptance is price action that we must see prior to entry, in order to prevent us from rushing in and getting trapped. It generally inv...

Remember the follow through on Failed Breakdowns will vary enormously, and we have 0 ability to predict how far any Failed Breakdown will go. Generally though, the return profile for a Failed Breakdown will approximate a statistical normal distribution. This means if you take 100 Failed Breakdowns, you will see an entire healthy distribution of returns. O...

In this case, we had (as usual) multiple entry types to choose from. We had a fairly subtle example of the first type of acceptance shown above. Remember the first form price back-tests the significant low (6872) from below, sells off, then returns to it. By doing this (selling off at the significant low then rebounding back to it), price tells us there i...

Remember 5 points above the significant low is the danger zone for Failed Breakdowns and where most losses occur. If entering in this zone you need clear acceptance and if you don’t see it or it is too noisy or uncertain, just wait for the non-acceptance protocol.

source_quote: They occur when price puts in one Failed Breakdown (the 9:42AM Failed Breakdown of yesterday’s 9:30AM 6961 low) and rallies nicely as we did to 6893. Then, ES loses the low of that original Failed Breakdown (which was 6857 this morning), recovers it, and rallies again. Now and then, we even see triple or quadruple dip Failed Breakdowns.

prose_context.text: Trade Recap/Daily Summary

The first low quality possible entry was down was a Failed Breakdown of 7355. I wrote yesterday at 4pm: “7345 is below there. This is also a shallow support. If its a slow grind into the zone one can buy it, but if we are free falling, don’t knife catch. A much safer entry is to wait for 7345 to defend, then recover 7355 to get long. Why 7355? At 12:30PM ...
Why was 7355 flush and recovery a Failed Breakdown? Remember a Failed Breakdown requires price to lose and recover a significant low.

A significant low has three possible definitions. 1) The prior days low 2) A multi-hour low/ a low that goes 20+ points or 3) A cluster or shelf of lows. At 7355 we had number #2: A clear low that was set at 12:30PM yesterday from which we rallied to the high of day ~7390.

article_title: [Re-Send] Bulls Bought The SPX Dip Yesterday. Will There Be Another This Week? May 6th Plan

prose_context.text: 1) Readers know that I talk everyday about the two siblings in ES. These siblings are elevator down sell and short squeeze, they are always paired together, and this is the tendency we exploit to generate our entries. Elevator down sell refers to a sharp flush where ES cuts through many supports with ease. This is usually how ES sells off. It rallies slow...

We saw this exactly this dynamic around 4:30PM after earnings last Wednesday evening. Earnings hit, and ES rapidly went elevator down from 7190 at 4pm, down to 7132 by 4:18PM. This is what an elevator down sell looks like. It is a straight drop that knifes through every support with ease and with no bounces. Most elevator down sells can cover 40,50,60 poi...

To summarize the trade management methodology, it therefore goes like this: Lock in 75% profits at first level up. From there, you can move your stop up, for me, it will usually go several points points under break-even, but I am flexible here. My only core criteria is that I *will not let the entire trade go back red*. I want to give the trade every chan...

We got this yesterday. We went elevator down from 7280’s at 11:20AM to 7198 by 12:15PM. Shortly after, ES recovered a major low at 7213 ( set at 6:20PM Monday morning) and ripped into today’s 7280+ highs.

prose_context.text: Remember the non-acceptance protocol automatically activates when price recovers the significant low (7220) by 5 points (7225) and holds at or above 7225 for a few minutes. The non-acceptance protocol typically triggers for shallow (under 10 point flush of the significant low) and fast Failed Breakdowns, but you can use it for any Failed Breakdown. Typica...

Remember 5 points above the significant low is the danger zone for Failed Breakdowns and where most losses occur. If entering in this zone you need clear acceptance and if you don’t see it or it is too noisy or uncertain, just wait for the non-acceptance protocol.

As always with Failed Breakdowns though the golden rule is if you just rush in long on the recovery of the significant low, you lose. Prior to entry you HAVE TO see either: 1) One of the two acceptance types. Acceptance is price action that we must see prior to entry, in order to prevent us from rushing in and getting trapped. Why was 7247 flush and recovery a Failed Breakdown? Remember a Failed Breakdown requires price to lose and recover a significant low.

A significant low has three possible definitions. 1) The prior days low 2) A multi-hour low/ a low that goes 20+ points or 3) A cluster or shelf of lows. At 7248 we had #1. 7248 was basically the daily low for Friday and it held for the entire session. The flush and recovery would be actionable.

Remember 5 points above the significant low is the danger zone for Failed Breakdowns and where most losses occur. If entering in this zone you need clear acceptance and if you don’t see it or it is too noisy or uncertain, just wait for the non-acceptance protocol.

As always with Failed Breakdowns though the golden rule is if you just rush in long on the recovery of the significant low, you lose. Prior to entry you HAVE TO see either: 1) One of the two acceptance types. Acceptance is price action that we must see prior to entry, in order to prevent us from rushing in and getting trapped. prose_context.text: Remember the non-acceptance protocol automatically activates when price recovers the significant low (7188) by 5 points (7193) and holds at or above 7193 for a few minutes. The non-acceptance protocol typically triggers for shallow (under 10 point flush of the significant low) and fast Failed Breakdowns, but you can use it for any Failed Breakdown. Typica...

Remember 5 points above the significant low is the danger zone for Failed Breakdowns and where most losses occur. If entering in this zone you need clear acceptance and if you don’t see it or it is too noisy or uncertain, just wait for the non-acceptance protocol.

As always with Failed Breakdowns though the golden rule is if you just rush in long on the recovery of the significant low, you lose. Prior to entry you HAVE TO see either: 1) One of the two acceptance types. Acceptance is price action that we must see prior to entry, in order to prevent us from rushing in and getting trapped. It generally involves price ...

prose_context.text: Remember the non-acceptance protocol automatically activates when price recovers the significant low (7188) by 5 points (7193) and holds at or above 7193 for a few minutes. The non-acceptance protocol typically triggers for shallow (under 10 point flush of the significant low) and fast Failed Breakdowns, but you can use it for any Failed Breakdown. Typica...

Remember 5 points above the significant low is the danger zone for Failed Breakdowns and where most losses occur. If entering in this zone you need clear acceptance and if you don’t see it or it is too noisy or uncertain, just wait for the non-acceptance protocol.

As always with Failed Breakdowns though the golden rule is if you just rush in long on the recovery of the significant low, you lose. Prior to entry you HAVE TO see either: 1) One of the two acceptance types. Acceptance is price action that we must see prior to entry, in order to prevent us from rushing in and getting trapped. It generally involves price ...

The basic context heading into today was twofold. Firstly - at 2:58PM yesterday - ES flushed a multitouch shelf of lows at 7147 down to 7137, and recovered. 7147 was support of a consolidation or what I call a Mode 2 Range that had been in play for 48hrs 7147-7189, discussed above.

Remember Mode 2 is the default state of price action and refers to a session where price does not trend one way from open to close. Mode 2 days are rangebound sessions and about 90% of days are Mode 2. Mode 2 contrasts with Mode 1. Mode 1 days are days where price trends one way from open to close. These are very rare

prose_context.text: Why was 7137 flush and recovery a Failed Breakdown? Remember a Failed Breakdown requires price to lose and recover a significant low.

The first high quality possible entry was down was a Failed Breakdown of 7137. I wrote yesterday at 330PM: “7147 is first support down of interest. This level is totally washed out now and I would never buy it. The only setup is interest is the Failed Breakdown of today’s low which is at 7137. If we can trap this and recover, one can try the long. If you ...

A significant low has three possible definitions. 1) The prior days low 2) A multi-hour low/ a low that goes 20+ points or 3) A cluster or shelf of lows. At 7137 we had #1. This was the low of day for Wednesday.

Ranges like this are what I call Mode 2. Mode 2 is easy to trade as long as you follow the rules. 1) Do not predict price action. Do not predict what path price will take to fill out the range, when it will breakout, or how it will breakout. If you try to predict, you’ve lost already. Simply plan triggers, react. 2) Always manage trades level to level. In...

prose_context.text: Remember the non-acceptance protocol automatically activates when price recovers the significant low (7137) by 5 points (7142) and holds at or above 7142 for a few minutes. The non-acceptance protocol typically triggers for shallow (under 10 point flush of the significant low) and fast Failed Breakdowns, but you can use it for any Failed Breakdown. Typica...

Around 4:00PM, earnings trapping began. I wrote yesterday at 4pm: “Note we have earnings at 4pm so expect fast, wild, trappy, algo driven swings.” We trapped up to ~7189 range resistance, then trapped down to 7132 by 4:20PM. Just after that, we got the 7137 Failed Breakdown discussed above.

Remember 5 points above the significant low is the danger zone for Failed Breakdowns and where most losses occur. If entering in this zone you need clear acceptance and if you don’t see it or it is too noisy or uncertain, just wait for the non-acceptance protocol.

As I wrote yesterday at 4pm: “My general lean is we fill this out (perhaps trap support once more) then see 7174, 7189. Perhaps dip there, then breakout to 7204, 7220, 7238/48. Bear case discussed above.”

article_title: SPX Is Coiled Tight Again. Is Another Move Coming? What Way? April 30 Plan

prose_context.text: 1) Readers know that I talk everyday about the two siblings in ES. These siblings are elevator down sell and short squeeze, they are always paired together, and this is the tendency we exploit to generate our entries. Elevator down sell refers to a sharp flush where ES cuts through many supports with ease. This is usually how ES sells off. It rallies slow...

We saw this exactly on Thursday last week. ES went elevator down around 1pm. This elevator down sell took ES rapidly from 7160s at 1pm down to 7080. Shortly after - around 1:50PM - ES recovered a big shelf of lows at 7085 comprising the Sunday last week and Tuesday last week lows. This was a big Failed Breakdown, and we ripped from there to 7200+ on Friday.

To summarize the trade management methodology, it therefore goes like this: Lock in 75% profits at first level up. From there, you can move your stop up, for me, it will usually go several points points under break-even, but I am flexible here. My only core criteria is that I *will not let the entire trade go back red*. I want to give the trade every chan...

Then off the Sunday open? We got the same drill again (and the same drill as every Sunday for the last 6 weeks). Secondly, ES spent yesterday inside a tight Mode 2 range mostly 7147 to 7180. Remember Mode 2 is the default state of price action and refers to a session where price does not trend one way from open to close. Mode 2 days are rangebound sessions and about 90% of days are Mode 2. Mode 2 contrasts with Mode 1. Mode 1 days are days where price trends one way...

Remember 5 points above the significant low is the danger zone for Failed Breakdowns and where most losses occur. If entering in this zone you need clear acceptance and if you don’t see it or it is too noisy or uncertain, just wait for the non-acceptance protocol.

As always with Failed Breakdowns though the golden rule is if you just rush in long on the recovery of the significant low, you lose. I need to see a vertical “elevator down” sell. Then I long when price flushes a low/shelf of lows for a Failed Breakdown. That is it. I don’t short, I don’t chase longs, I don’t flip back and forth long and short, I don’t trade chop, I don’t trade low vol, I don’t trade because some influencer posted a trade, I don’t trade because of news. I don’t care if...

By 7:50PM, we popped above and one could have longed. I did not take this one but it worked for a level to level move for those who did. As stated we had very extensive acceptance here yesterday of the first type shown above. Recall the first form price back-tests the significant low from below, sells off, then returns to it. By doing this (selling off at...

Remember the non-acceptance protocol automatically activates when price recovers the significant low (7147) by 5 points (7152) and holds at or above 7152 for a few minutes. The non-acceptance protocol typically triggers for shallow (under 10 point flush of the significant low) and fast Failed Breakdowns, but you can use it for any Failed Breakdown. Typica...

article_title: FOMC + Earnings Tomorrow. Expect Volatility In SPX. Will The Rally Continue? April 29 Plan

prose_context.text: 1) Readers know that I talk everyday about the two siblings in ES. These siblings are elevator down sell and short squeeze, they are always paired together, and this is the tendency we exploit to generate our entries. Elevator down sell refers to a sharp flush where ES cuts through many supports with ease. This is usually how ES sells off. It rallies slow...

We saw this exactly on Thursday last week. ES went elevator down around 1pm. This elevator down sell took ES rapidly from 7160s at 1pm down to 7080. Shortly after - around 1:50PM - ES recovered a big shelf of lows at 7085 comprising the Sunday last week and Tuesday last week lows. This was a big Failed Breakdown, and we ripped from there to 7200+ on Friday.

Then off the Sunday open? We got the same drill again (and the same drill as every Sunday for the last 6 weeks). ES went elevator down into gap down off the Sunday futures open selling from 7190’s into 7172. Then last evening, ES recovered 7185 (a big shelf of lows set during the day Friday), and put in an actionable scalp to 7208 Sunday evening and stren...
Then overnight into Tuesday morning? ES went elevator down from 7224 to 7147 by 830AM. We bounced 30 points there, then at 10:50AM, swept that shelf (Failed Breakdown) and rallied into the close.

source_quote: Heading into today, I verified my positioning at the close yesterday at 4pm: “I am still holding my 10% long runner from the 1:50PM Thursday Failed Breakdown of 7085 - discussed extensively in Thursday’s newsletter and provided in advance. My most recent entry was the 840PM 7185 Failed Breakdown from Sunday evening.”

Remember 5 points above the significant low is the danger zone for Failed Breakdowns and where most losses occur. If entering in this zone you need clear acceptance and if you don’t see it or it is too noisy or uncertain, just wait for the non-acceptance protocol.

article_title: Is the Next Leg Higher About To Start In SPX? Or Pullback Soon? April 28 Plan

prose_context.text: 1) Readers know that I talk everyday about the two siblings in ES. These siblings are elevator down sell and short squeeze, they are always paired together, and this is the tendency we exploit to generate our entries. Elevator down sell refers to a sharp flush where ES cuts through many supports with ease. This is usually how ES sells off. It rallies slow...

We saw this exactly on Thursday. ES went elevator down around 1pm. This elevator down sell took ES rapidly from 7160s at 1pm down to 7080. Shortly after - around 1:50PM - ES recovered a big shelf of lows at 7085 comprising the Sunday last week and Tuesday last week lows. This was a big Failed Breakdown, and we ripped from there to 7200+ on Friday.

To summarize the trade management methodology, it therefore goes like this: Lock in 75% profits at first level up. From there, you can move your stop up, for me, it will usually go several points points under break-even, but I am flexible here. My only core criteria is that I *will not let the entire trade go back red*. I want to give the trade every chan...

Then off the Sunday open? We got the same drill again (and the same drill as every Sunday for the last 6 weeks). ES went elevator down into gap down off the Sunday futures open selling from 7190’s into 7172. Then last evening, ES recovered 7185 (a big shelf of lows set during the day Friday), and put in an actionable scalp to 7208 Sunday evening.

These siblings are elevator down sell and short squeeze, they are always paired together, and this is the tendency we exploit to generate our entries. Elevator down sell refers to a sharp flush where ES cuts through many supports with ease. This is usually how ES sells off. It rallies slow...

To summarize the trade management methodology, it therefore goes like this: Lock in 75% profits at first level up. From there, you can move your stop up, for me, it will usually go several points points under break-even, but I am flexible here. My only core criteria is that I *will not let the entire trade go back red*. I want to give the trade every chan...

A little after the close Tuesday, ES recovered a major low at 7097 (this low held for much of the day Tuesday), initiating a Failed Breakdown in the process. From there, we got the short squeeze to 7160+ Wednesday.

Then Wednesday evening? A little after 8pm we saw a classic Failed Breakdown. Driven by an erroneous headline, ES went elevator down a little after 8pm from 7160’s to 7105. In doing so, ES lost a major low at 7129 from Wednesday morning. Shortly after, that 7129 low recovered, and ES ripped to 7180 by noon Wednesday.

article_title: A Full Week Of Coil For SPX. Do We Trend Next Week? April 27 Pan

prose_context.text: The basic context heading into today was that ES remained stuck inside a “Mode 2 Range”. Recall that 90% of the time price is in Mode 2 - this refers to rangebound price action where price does not trend in one direction from open to close. It traps bulls, it traps bears, and traps those looking for straight lines. This has been all week. The other 10% of...
Trade Recap/Daily Summary

Ranges like this are what I call Mode 2. Mode 2 is easy to trade as long as you follow the rules. 1) Do not predict price action. Do not predict what path price will take to fill out the range, when it will breakout, or how it will breakout. If you try to predict, you’ve lost already. article_title: SPX Continues To Coil Tighter. Is A Breakout Close? April 23rd Plan

prose_context.text: Trade Recap/Daily Summary
Why was 7097 flush and recovery a Failed Breakdown? Remember a Failed Breakdown requires price to lose and recover a significant low.

A significant low has three possible definitions. 1) The prior days low 2) A multi-hour low/ a low that goes 20+ points or 3) A cluster or shelf of lows. At 7097 we had #1: A the daily low for Tuesday. Specifically, at 1:30PM Tuesday ES bottomed there, rallied 41 points, and this acted as the low for much of the day.

Around 3:50PM yesterday, ES flushed yesterday’s 7097 low (this was a big low that held most the session) down to 7085 and recovered for a Failed Breakdown from which we ripped back up into that range.

article_title: SPX Continues To Coil Tighter. Is A Breakout Close? April 23rd Plan

prose_context.text: Why was 7097 flush and recovery a Failed Breakdown? Remember a Failed Breakdown requires price to lose and recover a significant low.

A significant low has three possible definitions. 1) The prior days low 2) A multi-hour low/ a low that goes 20+ points or 3) A cluster or shelf of lows. At 7097 we had #1: A the daily low for Tuesday. Specifically, at 1:30PM Tuesday ES bottomed there, rallied 41 points, and this acted as the low for much of the day.

As always with Failed Breakdowns though the golden rule is if you just rush in long on the recovery of the significant low, you lose. Prior to entry you HAVE TO see either: 1) One of the two acceptance types. Acceptance is price action that we must see prior to entry, in order to prevent us from rushing in and getting trapped. It generally involves price ...

Remember 5 points above the significant low is the danger zone for Failed Breakdowns and where most losses occur. If entering in this zone you need clear acceptance and if you don’t see it or it is too noisy or uncertain, just wait for the non-acceptance protocol.

source_quote: The first possible entry was down was a Failed Breakdown of 7097. I wrote yesterday at 4pm: “Instead the much safer entry is to wait for a Failed Breakdown of today’s low which is at 7097. Let that flush and recover. Ideally here wait for 7105 to clear before entry though (this is where the non-acceptance protocol entry would be anyway).”

article_title: After 3 Weeks of Upside, SPX Is Coiled. Another Breakout Coming? April 22nd Plan

prose_context.text: Trade Recap/Daily Summary

The first possible entry was down was a Failed Breakdown of 7120. I wrote yesterday at 4pm: “Below there is 7121. There is a massive shelf of lows at 7121. Specifically at 11am we set a huge low there and rallied to 7147+. At 730AM, we set another low there and rallied 37+ points. This is a clear zone we’d want to trade the flush and recovery of and a Fai...
Why was 7120 flush and recovery a Failed Breakdown? Remember a Failed Breakdown requires price to lose and recover a significant low.

A significant low has three possible definitions. 1) The prior days low 2) A multi-hour low/ a low that goes 20+ points or 3) A cluster or shelf of lows. At 7120 we had #3: A shelf of lows. Specifically, at 730am and 11am yesterday ES set big lows at 7120 from which we rallied 37 and 30 points respectively. The flush and recovery here is actionable.

The non-acceptance protocol typically triggers for shallow (under 10 point flush of the significant low) and fast Failed Breakdowns, but you can use it for any Failed Breakdown. Typica...

Unfortunately, ES never got this and we simply continued higher directly. I wrote yesterday at 4pm: “The bull case is that bulls can simply fill out this structure more (entries described above) then break it out. Breakout would target 7160, 7174, 7194, then 7217.”

Remember 5 points above the significant low is the danger zone for Failed Breakdowns and where most losses occur. If entering in this zone you need clear acceptance and if you don’t see it or it is too noisy or uncertain, just wait for the non-acceptance protocol.

prose_context.text: Why was 7120 flush and recovery a Failed Breakdown? Remember a Failed Breakdown requires price to lose and recover a significant low.

A significant low has three possible definitions. 1) The prior days low 2) A multi-hour low/ a low that goes 20+ points or 3) A cluster or shelf of lows. At 7120 we had #2: A multi-hour 20+ point low. At 9:45AM Friday ES put in a massive 20+ point rally from which it rallied to the high of day.

As always with Failed Breakdowns though the golden rule is if you just rush in long on the recovery of the significant low, you lose. Prior to entry you HAVE TO see either: 1) One of the two acceptance types. Acceptance is price action that we must see prior to entry, in order to prevent us from rushing in and getting trapped. It generally involves price ...

Remember 5 points above the significant low is the danger zone for Failed Breakdowns and where most losses occur. If entering in this zone you need clear acceptance and if you don’t see it or it is too noisy or uncertain, just wait for the non-acceptance protocol.

prose_context.text: Trade Recap/Daily Summary

The first possible entry was down was a Failed Breakdown of 7058. I wrote yesterday at 4pm: “First support as of writing though is 7058. We set two small lows there today one at 1:10PM and one at 2:20PM. Instead of buying this directly, its best to wait for that shelf of lows to flush and recover as a Failed Breakdown. 7048 is below there. This is where w...
Why was 7058 flush and recovery a Failed Breakdown? Remember a Failed Breakdown requires price to lose and recover a significant low.

A significant low has three possible definitions. 1) The prior days low 2) A multi-hour low/ a low that goes 20+ points or 3) A cluster or shelf of lows. At 7058 we had #3: A shelf of lows at 7058 set at 1:10PM and 2:20PM yesterday.

article_title: Is Buy The Dip Back In SPX? April 14 Plan

prose_context.text: The first possible entry was down was a Failed Breakdown of 6848-52. I wrote on Friday at 4pm: “6848-52 is first support down and this has been tested 5x in the last 24hrs with one small flush today. I would not engage this zone again as it is too well tested, but if we can flush today’s 6847 low (perhaps down to 6840) and recover, this would be an action...
Why was 6848-52 flush and recovery a Failed Breakdown? Remember a Failed Breakdown requires price to lose and recover a significant low.

A significant low has three possible definitions. 1) The prior days low 2) A multi-hour low/ a low that goes 20+ points or 3) A cluster or shelf of lows. At 6848-52 we had #2: A clear cluster or shelf of lows. Specifically between 1:40PM Thursday and 12:40PM Friday ES put in a clear 5 touch shelf of lows there. The flush and recovery of this shelf would b...

As always with Failed Breakdowns though the golden rule is if you just rush in long on the recovery of the significant low, you lose. Prior to entry you HAVE TO see either: 1) One of the two acceptance types. Acceptance is price action that we must see prior to entry, in order to prevent us from rushing in and getting trapped. It generally involves price ...

prose_context.text: Remember the non-acceptance protocol automatically activates when price recovers the significant low (6848) by 5 points (6853) and holds at or above 6853 for a few minutes. The non-acceptance protocol typically triggers for shallow (under 10 point flush of the significant low) and fast Failed Breakdowns, but you can use it for any Failed Breakdown. Typica...

Remember 5 points above the significant low is the danger zone for Failed Breakdowns and where most losses occur. If entering in this zone you need clear acceptance and if you don’t see it or it is too noisy or uncertain, just wait for the non-acceptance protocol.

prose_context.text: 1) Readers know that I talk everyday about the two siblings in ES. These siblings are elevator down sell and short squeeze, they are always paired together, and this is the tendency we exploit to generate our entries. Elevator down sell refers to a sharp flush where ES cuts through many supports with ease. This is usually how ES sells off. It rallies slow...

To summarize the trade management methodology, it therefore goes like this: Lock in 75% profits at first level up. From there, you can move your stop up, for me, it will usually go several points points under break-even, but I am flexible here. My only core criteria is that I *will not let the entire trade go back red*. I want to give the trade every chan...

We went elevator down nearly 100 points to 6572 by 11am Tuesday. Shortly after, we put in a Failed Breakdown of 6595 (at 11:15PM on Sunday April 5th ES put in a large bounce at 6595 to 6560s making it a clear significant low of the sort we would want to trade a loss and recovery of) and we ripped to 6647+ after to set the high of day for Tuesday’s regular...

From there? Gap down/Elevator down Sunday to 6760s. As always, this would resolve with a short squeeze, but only when we get a Failed Breakdown. As I will discuss below, at around 8:45PM last evening ES recovered a 5-touch shelf of lows at 6793 from last Wednesday/Thursday, and we ripped from there to 6900+ today.

article_title: Is Buy The Dip Back In SPX? April 14 Plan

prose_context.text: The first possible entry was down was a Failed Breakdown of 6848-52. I wrote on Friday at 4pm: “6848-52 is first support down and this has been tested 5x in the last 24hrs with one small flush today. I would not engage this zone again as it is too well tested, but if we can flush today’s 6847 low (perhaps down to 6840) and recover, this would be an action...
Why was 6848-52 flush and recovery a Failed Breakdown? Remember a Failed Breakdown requires price to lose and recover a significant low.

A significant low has three possible definitions. 1) The prior days low 2) A multi-hour low/ a low that goes 20+ points or 3) A cluster or shelf of lows. At 6848-52 we had #2: A clear cluster or shelf of lows. Specifically between 1:40PM Thursday and 12:40PM Friday ES put in a clear 5 touch shelf of lows there. The flush and recovery of this shelf would b...

As always with Failed Breakdowns though the golden rule is if you just rush in long on the recovery of the significant low, you lose. Prior to entry you HAVE TO see either: 1) One of the two acceptance types. Acceptance is price action that we must see prior to entry, in order to prevent us from rushing in and getting trapped. It generally involves price ...

At 6848-52 we had #2: A clear cluster or shelf of lows. Specifically between 1:40PM Thursday and 12:40PM Friday ES put in a clear 5 touch shelf of lows there. The flush and recovery of this shelf would b...
Why was 6848-52 flush and recovery a Failed Breakdown? Remember a Failed Breakdown requires price to lose and recover a significant low.

As always with Failed Breakdowns though the golden rule is if you just rush in long on the recovery of the significant low, you lose. Prior to entry you HAVE TO see either: 1) One of the two acceptance types. Acceptance is price action that we must see prior to entry, in order to prevent us from rushing in and getting trapped. It generally involves price ...

The first possible entry was down was a Failed Breakdown of 6848-52. I wrote on Friday at 4pm: “6848-52 is first support down and this has been tested 5x in the last 24hrs with one small flush today. I would not engage this zone again as it is too well tested, but if we can flush today’s 6847 low (perhaps down to 6840) and recover, this would be an action...

prose_context.text: Remember the non-acceptance protocol automatically activates when price recovers the significant low (6848) by 5 points (6853) and holds at or above 6853 for a few minutes. The non-acceptance protocol typically triggers for shallow (under 10 point flush of the significant low) and fast Failed Breakdowns, but you can use it for any Failed Breakdown. Typica...

Remember 5 points above the significant low is the danger zone for Failed Breakdowns and where most losses occur. If entering in this zone you need clear acceptance and if you don’t see it or it is too noisy or uncertain, just wait for the non-acceptance protocol.

article_title: Massive Breakout Underway For SPX. How Much More Upside? April 10 Plan

prose_context.text: 1) Readers know that I talk everyday about the two siblings in ES. These siblings are elevator down sell and short squeeze, they are always paired together, and this is the tendency we exploit to generate our entries. Elevator down sell refers to a sharp flush where ES cuts through many supports with ease. This is usually how ES sells off. It rallies slow...

We saw exactly this off the Sunday open. At 6pm futures gapped down from 6615+, then went elevator down to 6565 or so. Shortly after - around 8pm - ES recovered 6593 which was a huge shelf of lows from Thursday and to a lesser extent Friday. This was a Failed Breakdown and from there, we short squeezed to 6660+ Monday high of day.

To summarize the trade management methodology, it therefore goes like this: Lock in 75% profits at first level up. From there, you can move your stop up, for me, it will usually go several points points under break-even, but I am flexible here. My only core criteria is that I *will not let the entire trade go back red*. I want to give the trade every chan...

Then Tuesday? The cycle restarts. We went elevator down nearly 100 points to 6572 by 11am Tuesday. Shortly after, we put in a Failed Breakdown of 6595 (at 11:15PM on Sunday ES put in a large bounce at 6595 to 6560s making it a clear significant low) and we ripped to 6647+ after to set the high of day for Tuesday’s regular hours. Then Tuesday evening, this...

We saw exactly this off the Sunday open. At 6pm futures gapped down from 6615+, then went elevator down to 6565 or so. Shortly after - around 8pm - ES recovered 6593 which was a huge shelf of lows from Thursday and to a lesser extent Friday. This was a Failed Breakdown and from there, we short squeezed to 6660+ Monday high of day.

1) Readers know that I talk everyday about the two siblings in ES. These siblings are elevator down sell and short squeeze, they are always paired together, and this is the tendency we exploit to generate our entries. Elevator down sell refers to a sharp flush where ES cuts through many supports with ease. This is usually how ES sells off. It rallies slow...

2) The basic theme heading into today was that last Monday March 30th at 8:30PM ES put in a monster Failed Breakdown at the 6360 level. All major rallies and institutional accumulation events start this way, and last weeks was no different. We had a shelf of lows there (Sunday’s March 29 low & Monday’s March 39th regular hours low) at 6360. We flushed tha...

article_title: Massive Breakout Underway For SPX. How Much More Upside? April 10 Plan

prose_context.text: Trade Recap/Daily Summary

The first possible entry was a Level Reclaim long of 6809. I wrote yesterday at 3:30pm: “In terms of spots to add on strength, I would stick to the above entries but one possible trade is the reclaim of 6809. 6809 was a clear support/resistance shelf for the last 12hrs. Resistance from 720PM to 11pm Tuesday evening. Support from 12:20PM to 2pm Wednesday m...

This was an example of a Level Reclaim Long as opposed to a Failed Breakdown. Recall that Level Reclaims are an advanced, rarer variant of the Failed Breakdown. The Failed Breakdown is 90% of my trades and the setup I discuss above and daily. Failed Breakdowns are simple: Price sets a clear significant low or cluster of lows, sweeps it (loses it), recover...

A significant low has a precise definition and refers to one of three things: 1. The prior days low 2. A multi-hour low/ a low that goes 20+ points or 3. A cluster or shelf of lows. We can long when the low recovers, after we see a condition called acceptance and I discuss this in enormous detail in my methodology page section 9.

source_quote: We closed up yesterday around 6815 and the runner from Tuesday’s 6593 Failed Breakdown continued to work. This meant - for me - there was little to do but hold my runner and wait for the next elevator down sell.

article_title: Massive Breakout Underway For SPX. How Much More Upside? April 10 Plan

prose_context.text: Remember the non-acceptance protocol automatically activates when price recovers the significant low (6809) by 5 points (6814) and holds at or above 6814 for a few minutes. The non-acceptance protocol typically triggers for shallow (under 10 point flush of the significant low) and fast Failed Breakdowns, but you can use it for any Failed Breakdown. Typica...

Remember 5 points above the significant low is the danger zone for Failed Breakdowns and where most losses occur. If entering in this zone you need clear acceptance and if you don’t see it or it is too noisy or uncertain, just wait for the non-acceptance protocol.

As always with Failed Breakdowns OR Level Reclaims though, the golden rule is if you just rush in long on the recovery of the significant low, you lose. Prior to entry you HAVE TO see either: 1) One of the two acceptance types. Acceptance is price action that we must see prior to entry, in order to prevent us from rushing in and getting trapped. It genera...

When I woke up and checked price at 730AM almost nothing happened overnight and we were still in deep, low volatility consolidation. I wrote yesterday at 4pm: “6793 is 1st support down. At around 3pm ES set a nice low there and bounced ~20 points. The Failed Breakdown of this low is actionable.”

We saw exactly this Monday evening. Monday evening ES went elevator down from 6392 at 6pm to 6353 by 820pm. Shortly after, ES put in a Failed Breakdown of Sunday’s 6360 daily low AND Monday’s 6360 regular hours low which was located in the same spot, and we ripped a staggering 280+ points into 6647 yesterday.

This is the power of the Failed Breakdown: Price sets a shelf of lows (6360), traps shorts and runs stops below it, recovers, and we rip parabolically. Not every failed breakdown works this well though - some just go 5 or 10 points then fail. That is fine too. We don’t need to predict which is which. We just manage level to level, leave a runner, price do...

prose_context.text: Trade Recap/Daily Summary

The first possible entry was the Failed Breakdown of 6603. I wrote yesterday at 4pm: “We therefore set a clear significant low here. Instead of bidding 6603, the safest bet is to wait for the Failed Breakdown. If we can flush 6603 to 6593 (a big support in itself) and recover, this is a good entry.”
Remember a Failed Breakdown requires price to lose and recover a significant low.

A significant low has three possible definitions. 1) The prior days low 2) A multi-hour low/ a low that goes 20+ points or 3) A cluster or shelf of lows. At 6603 we had #2. We put in a nice bounce here at 2:30PM yesterday, bouncing ~30 points. Therefore, the flush and recovery of this would be actionable.

prose_context.text: 6604 was first support down after 4pm yesterday. I wrote yesterday at 4pm: “6604 is first support down as of writing. This is a zone we have tested many times today. While one could buy it directly, a much safer choice is to wait for a Failed Breakdown of the 1:40PM 6598 low. This was a big low from which we rallied 43 points, making it a significant low....
Remember a Failed Breakdown requires price to lose and recover a significant low.
Trade Recap/Daily Summary

A significant low has three possible definitions. 1) The prior days low 2) A multi-hour low/ a low that goes 20+ points or 3) A cluster or shelf of lows. At 6598 we had #2. At 1:40PM on Tuesday, ES set a low there from which we rallied 43 points. This is a clear, V-shaped low of the sort we’d want to see a flush and recovery of.

prose_context.text: Heading into today, I was still holding a 10% long runner from the 10:30AM 6635 (March 8th major low) Failed Breakdown we saw yesterday. I confirmed this at the close yesterday, stating: “I am holding my 10% long runner still from the ~10:30AM 6635 Failed Breakdown today and this was a very choppy region which I discuss more about above. “

1) Readers know that I talk everyday about the two siblings in ES. These siblings are elevator down sell and short squeeze, they are always paired together, and this is the tendency we exploit to generate our entries. Elevator down sell refers to a sharp flush where ES cuts through many supports with ease. This is usually how ES sells off. It rallies slow...

prose_context.text: For those eager to trade though, there was after 4pm yesterday a “Level Reclaim” long available above 6658-63. I provided this yesterday at 4pm when I wrote: “I won’t be adding up here as I have my runner, but one possible complex entry is the 6658-6663 reclaim. 6658-63 is a major support/resistance line. It was the Sunday opening low, it was the Wednesda...
Trade Recap/Daily Summary

This was an example of a Level Reclaim Long as opposed to a Failed Breakdown. Recall that Level Reclaims are an advanced, rarer variant of the Failed Breakdown. The Failed Breakdown is 90% of my trades and the setup I discuss above and daily. Failed Breakdowns are simple: Price sets a clear significant low or cluster of lows, sweeps it (loses it), recover...

A significant low has a precise definition and refers to one of three things: 1. The prior days low 2. A multi-hour low/ a low that goes 20+ points or 3. A cluster or shelf of lows. We can long when the low recovers, after we see a condition called acceptance and I discuss this in enormous detail in my methodology page section 9.

source_quote: We closed up after a big rally (with the most recent trigger being 10:30AM yesterday Failed Breakdown of the March 8th 6635 low. I expanded on this in great detail yesterday. There was little for me to do from here.

prose_context.text: Rememeber the non-acceptance protocol automatically activates when price recovers the significant low (6663) by 5 points (6669) and holds at or above 6669 for a few minutes. The non-acceptance protocol typically triggers for shallow (under 10 point flush of the significant low) and fast Failed Breakdowns, but you can use it for any Failed Breakdown. Typic...
Friday Morning and the 6612 Thursday Daily Low Failed Breakdown

Remember 5 points above the significant low is the danger zone for Failed Breakdowns and where most losses occur. If entering in this zone you need clear acceptance and if you don’t see it or it is too noisy or uncertain, just wait for the non-acceptance protocol.

As always with Failed Breakdowns or Level Reclaims though the golden rule is if you just rush in long on the recovery of the significant low, you lose. Prior to entry you HAVE TO see either: 1) One of the two acceptance types. Acceptance is price action that we must see prior to entry, in order to prevent us from rushing in and getting trapped. The failed breakout/breakdown setup is premised on the fact that the vast majority of breakouts and breakdowns are traps. Price absolutely loves to break down a support, trap those chasing, then reverse and squeeze the other way. Breakouts are therefore exceptionally hard to trade, and take an enormous amount of skill and screen time to recognize the cond...

Institutions have a set bag of tricks they use to enter the market, and the failed breakdown is #1 on that list. If you want to enter with enormous size, you need liquidity, and to find liquidity, you have to hunt for it. Fortunately for institutions, liquidity hides in very predictable spots (and it just so happens to be the spots that most retail trader...

1) Price must put in a significant low. This is the most important criteria and if this is not present, there is no Failed Breakdown. A significant low is ideally the prior days low and this is the gold standard. Absent this, a major multi-hour low can work, provided it goes 20-30+ points. Absent this, a cluster of lows can also work. It cannot be any ran...

2) The low should occur at a technically significant spot. Ideally, a major trendline or horizontal support or prior area where price broke out and is now back-testing. It can’t just be any random spot on the chart and must be in an important area that was previously significant if you look to the left of the chart.

article_title: My Trade Methodology - Fundamentals

prose_context.text: What is a Level Reclaim then? A Level Reclaim has none of that. A Level Reclaim is when ES recovers a horizontal resistance line or shelf, that often also previously acted as support. I call these “S/R Shelves”. This will typically be a horizontal line which had a touch or two as support, then failed, and thereafter had a few touches as resistance. These ...

Level Reclaims are hard because you need to know how to chart horizontal lines. With Failed Breakdowns you don’t - you just need to be able to spot a significant low. When it comes to entry on Level Reclaims, one can just follow the same requirements as a Traditional Failed Breakdown (wait for acceptance, then long the recovery).

When looking to take a trade based on this - patience is the necessary condition. Acceptance takes time and almost always there are many actionable trades *during* the acceptance process. In Mode 2, price picks a range, and bulls and bears are generally in balance, with one side typically slightly favored depending on the underlying trend. Mode 2 is characterized by traps, lack of follow through, and indecision - the exact opposite of Mode 1. In Mode 2, failed breakdowns are your...

My trading, and all professional trading is about reacting to price action - not predicting it. For a week straight now, ES has been clearly rangebound mostly stuck between 6925 resistance and 6849 support. As I’ve frequently discussed, one benefit of trading ES is its highl...

prose_context.text: 1) Readers know that I talk everyday about the two siblings in ES. These siblings are elevator down sell and short squeeze, they are always paired together, and this is the tendency we exploit to generate our entries. Elevator down sell refers to a sharp flush where ES cuts through many supports with ease. This is usually how ES sells off. It rallies slow...

We saw precisely this today in the morning around 9:30AM. ES went rapidly elevator down selling from 6865 to 6828. In doing so though, it lost the Monday 6832 daily low by a few points. It almost immediately recovered that low (Failed Breakdown) and we we ripped higher to 6890+. You can see the whole sequence: A fast, vicious flush lower that trapped bear...

To summarize the trade management methodology, it therefore goes like this: Lock in 75% profits at first level up. From there, you can move your stop up, for me, it will usually go several points points under break-even, but I am flexible here. My only core criteria is that I *will not let the entire trade go back red*. I want to give the trade every chan...

prose_context.text: The Level Reclaim does not have this. Rather, the Level Reclaim involves ES recovering a clear horizontal support/resistance trendline. Instead of there being a low that sweeps, traps, and recovers like the Failed Breakdown, here we have a clear classical horizontal trendline. This is what we had at 6849. It was a clear support line last Tuesday, Thursday...

As always with Failed Breakdowns (or level reclaims) though the golden rule is if you just rush in long on the recovery of the significant low, you lose. Prior to entry you HAVE TO see either: 1) One of the two acceptance types. Acceptance is price action that we must see prior to entry, in order to prevent us from rushing in and getting trapped. It gener...

A significant low has a precise definition and refers to one of three things: 1. The prior days low 2. A multi-hour low/ a low that goes 20+ points or 3. A cluster or shelf of lows. We can long when the low recovers, after we see a condition called acceptance and I discuss this in enormous detail in my methodology page section 9.

Remember 5 points above the significant low is the danger zone for Failed Breakdowns and where most losses occur. If entering in this zone you need clear acceptance and if you don’t see it or it is too noisy or uncertain, just wait for the non-acceptance protocol.

Only 10% of days are trend days, and its impossible t...
